title: Trigonophyllum bicorne
taxonomy: Trigonophyllum bicorne belongs to the division Equisetophyta, which is a group of vascular plants that are closely related to horsetails and ferns. Within this division, Trigonophyllum bicorne is classified as a member of the class Equisetopsida. This classification is further narrowed down to the family Equisetaceae, which is a family of vascular plants that are characterized by their distinctive, hollow stems. Trigonophyllum bicorne is thought to have evolved during the Devonian period, which is approximately 416 to 359 million years ago. As a member of the class Equisetopsida, Trigonophyllum bicorne is considered to be an ancient plant that has survived for millions of years with minimal changes to its basic body plan.

intro: Trigonophyllum bicorne, also known as Bicornis plant, is a species of flowering plant in the family Selaginellaceae, characterized by its unique, two-horned leaves. This plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, and its health benefits include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which have been shown to aid in wound healing and reduce oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Trigonophyllum bicorne include flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Trigonophyllum bicorne is commonly prepared into teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being used in topical ointments and creams for skin conditions and wounds.
